title: The Magicians of Caprona — Chapter 3 (Fragment 2)
sourceText:
This was disturbing enough. And it seemed to be plain fact. All the years Paolo and Tonino had been at school, they had grown used to hearing that there had been this concession to Florence; that Pisa had demanded that agreement over fishing rights; or that Siena had raised taxes on imports to Caprona. They had grown too used to it to notice. But now it all seemed ominous. And worse shortly followed. News came that the Old Bridge had been seriously cracked by the winter floods.

This news caused the Casa Montana real dismay. For that bridge should have held. If it gave, it meant that the Montana charms in the foundations had given too. Aunt Francesca ran shrieking into the yard. “Those degenerate Petrocchis! They can’t even sustain an old spell now! We’ve been betrayed!”

Though no one else put it quite that way, Aunt Francesca probably spoke for the whole family.

As if that was not enough, Rinaldo set off that evening to visit his English girl, and was led back to the Casa streaming with blood, supported by his cousins Carlo and Giovanni. Rinaldo, using curse words Paolo and Tonino had never heard before, was understood to say he had met some Petrocchis. He had called them degenerate. And it was Aunt Maria’s turn to rush shrieking through the yard, shouting dire things about the Petrocchis. Rinaldo was the apple of Aunt Maria’s eye.

Rinaldo had been bandaged and put to bed, when Antonio and Uncle Lorenzo came back from viewing the damage to the Old Bridge. Both looked very serious. Old Guido Petrocchi himself had been there, with the Duke’s contractor, Mr. Andretti. Some very deep charms had given. It was going to take the whole of both families, working in shifts, at least three weeks to mend them.

“We could have used Rinaldo’s help,” Antonio said.

Rinaldo swore that he was well enough to get out of bed and help the next day, but Aunt Maria would not hear of it. Nor would the doctor. So the rest of the family was divided into shifts, and work went on day and night. Paolo, Lucia and Corinna went to the bridge straight from school every day. Tonino did not. He was still too slow to be much use. But from what Paolo told him, he did not think he was missing much. Paolo simply could not keep up with the furious pace of the spells. He was put to running errands, like poor Cousin Domenico. Tonino felt very sympathetic towards Domenico. He was the opposite of his dashing brother Rinaldo in every way, and he could not keep up with the pace of things either.

Work had been going on, often in pouring rain, for nearly a week, when the Duke of Caprona summoned Old Niccolo to speak to him.

Old Niccolo stood in the yard and tore what was left of his hair. Tonino laid down his book (it was called Machines of Death and quite fascinating) and went to see if he could help.

“Ah, Tonino,” said Old Niccolo, looking at him with the face of a grieving baby, “I have gigantic problems. Everyone is needed on the Old Bridge, and that ass Rinaldo is lying in bed, and I have to go before the Duke with some of my family. The Petrocchis have been summoned too. We cannot appear less than they are, after all. Oh why did Rinaldo choose such a time to shout stupid insults?”

Tonino had no idea what to say, so he said, “Shall I get Benvenuto?”

“No, no,” said Old Niccolo, more upset than ever. “The Duchess cannot abide cats. Benvenuto is no use here. I shall have to take those who are no use on the bridge. You shall go, Tonino, and Paolo and Domenico, and I shall take your Uncle Umberto to look wise and weighty. Perhaps that way we shan’t look so very thin.”

This was perhaps not the most flattering of invitations, but Tonino and Paolo were delighted nevertheless. They were delighted even though it rained hard the next day, the drilling white rain of winter. The dawn shift came in from the Old Bridge under shiny umbrellas, damp and disgruntled. Instead of resting, they had to turn to and get the party ready for the Palace.

The Montana family coach was dragged from the coach-house to a spot under the gallery, where it was carefully dusted. It was a great black thing with glass windows and monster black wheels. The Montana winged horse was emblazoned in a green shield on its heavy doors. The rain continued to pour down. Paolo, who hated rain as much as the cats did, was glad the coach was real. The horses were not. They were four white cardboard cut-outs of horses, which were kept leaning against the wall of the coach-house. They were an economical idea of Old Niccolo’s father’s. As he said, real horses ate and needed exercise and took up space the family could live in. The coachman was another cardboard cut-out—for much the same reasons—but he was kept inside the coach.

words.definition: Causing worry or anxiety; unsettling.
words.example: This was disturbing enough.
words.exampleText: This was disturbing enough.

words.definition: A thing that is granted, especially in response to demands.
words.example: There had been this concession to Florence.
words.exampleText: There had been this concession to Florence.

words.definition: Giving the impression that something bad or unpleasant is going to happen.
words.example: But now it all seemed ominous.
words.exampleText: But now it all seemed ominous.

words.definition: Consternation and distress, typically caused by something unexpected.
words.example: This news caused the Casa Montana real dismay.
words.exampleText: This news caused the Casa Montana real dismay.

words.definition: To utter a high-pitched piercing sound or words, especially as an expression of terror, pain, or excitement.
words.example: Aunt Francesca ran shrieking into the yard.
words.exampleText: Aunt Francesca ran shrieking into the yard.

words.definition: Having declined to a lower state, especially in terms of morals or physical condition.
words.example: Those degenerate Petrocchis!
words.exampleText: Those degenerate Petrocchis!

words.definition: To strengthen or support physically or mentally; to keep up or maintain.
words.example: They can’t even sustain an old spell now!
words.exampleText: They can’t even sustain an old spell now!

words.definition: To have been disloyal to someone, typically by revealing secrets or being unfaithful.
words.example: We’ve been betrayed!
words.exampleText: We’ve been betrayed!

words.definition: An offensive word or phrase used to express anger or annoyance.
words.example: Rinaldo, using curse words Paolo and Tonino had never heard before.
words.exampleText: Rinaldo, using curse words Paolo and Tonino had never heard before.

words.definition: To say something very loudly.
words.example: Rinaldo chose such a time to shout stupid insults.
words.exampleText: Rinaldo chose such a time to shout stupid insults.

words.definition: A person or company that undertakes a contract to provide materials or labour to perform a service or do a job.
words.example: The Duke’s contractor, Mr. Andretti.
words.exampleText: The Duke’s contractor, Mr. Andretti.

words.definition: Magical objects or spells believed to have supernatural power.
words.example: The Montana charms in the foundations had given too.
words.exampleText: The Montana charms in the foundations had given too.

words.definition: To repair something that is broken or damaged.
words.example: It was going to take at least three weeks to mend them.
words.exampleText: It was going to take at least three weeks to mend them.

words.definition: Feeling, showing, or expressing sympathy.
words.example: Tonino felt very sympathetic towards Domenico.
words.exampleText: Tonino felt very sympathetic towards Domenico.

words.definition: Attractive, adventurous, and full of confidence.
words.example: He was the opposite of his dashing brother Rinaldo.
words.exampleText: He was the opposite of his dashing brother Rinaldo.

words.definition: Rain falling very heavily.
words.example: Work had been going on, often in pouring rain.
words.exampleText: Work had been going on, often in pouring rain.

words.definition: Called upon to appear, especially by an authority.
words.example: The Duke of Caprona summoned Old Niccolo to speak to him.
words.exampleText: The Duke of Caprona summoned Old Niccolo to speak to him.

words.definition: Of very great size or extent; huge or enormous.
words.example: I have gigantic problems.
words.exampleText: I have gigantic problems.

words.definition: Expressing deep sorrow, especially for someone who has died.
words.example: Looking at him with the face of a grieving baby.
words.exampleText: Looking at him with the face of a grieving baby.

words.definition: To tolerate or endure something.
words.example: The Duchess cannot abide cats.
words.exampleText: The Duchess cannot abide cats.

words.definition: Pleasing and complimentary, sometimes excessively so.
words.example: This was perhaps not the most flattering of invitations.
words.exampleText: This was perhaps not the most flattering of invitations.

words.definition: Feeling or showing great pleasure.
words.example: Tonino and Paolo were delighted nevertheless.
words.exampleText: Tonino and Paolo were delighted nevertheless.

words.definition: Very intense and penetrating, often used about rain or noise.
words.example: The drilling white rain of winter.
words.exampleText: The drilling white rain of winter.

words.definition: Slightly wet, often in an unpleasant way.
words.example: The dawn shift came in damp and disgruntled.
words.exampleText: The dawn shift came in damp and disgruntled.

words.definition: Angry or dissatisfied.
words.example: The dawn shift came in from the Old Bridge under shiny umbrellas, damp and disgruntled.
words.exampleText: The dawn shift came in from the Old Bridge under shiny umbrellas, damp and disgruntled.

words.definition: Giving good value or service in relation to the amount of money, time, or effort spent.
words.example: They were an economical idea of Old Niccolo’s father’s.
words.exampleText: They were an economical idea of Old Niccolo’s father’s.
